docs.text:
From Cartesian points to free vectors A Cartesian point is an ordered pair of real numbers. Let \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad Q=(q_1,q_2). \] The notation is deliberately type-sensitive: \((p_1,p_2)\) denotes a point ; \((P,Q)\) denotes an ordered pair of points ; \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) denotes the anchored vector determined by that ordered pair; \([v_1,v_2]\) denotes a free vector , an abstract displacement independent of its starting point; \([Q-P]\) denotes the free vector extracted from the ordinary coordinate difference of the two points; \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) denotes the point obtained by applying the free vector \(\mathbf v\) at \(P\) . Round and square brackets therefore carry different information. Round brackets describe points. Square brackets describe free vectors. Arithmetic on Cartesian point coordinates Because Cartesian points are represented by ordered pairs of numbers, their coordinate descriptions admit the usual coordinatewise arithmetic. Write \[ O=(0,0). \] Then \[  egin{aligned} P+Q&=(p_1+q_1,\,p_2+q_2),\ -P&=(-p_1,-p_2),\ Q-P&=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2),\ \lambda P&=(\lambda p_1,\lambda p_2). \end{aligned} \] These are operations on point coordinates, and their results are again written with round brackets. In particular, \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2) \] is still an ordinary coordinate pair. It records the two numerical changes needed to move from \(P\) to \(Q\) , but it has not yet been declared to be a free vector. Arithmetic on Cartesian points Compare coordinate arithmetic with the separate operation of translating a point by a vector. ⛶ Point addition Opposite point Point subtraction Scale point coordinates Point $\oplus$ vector $\lambda$ = 1.5 The ordinary operations use round coordinate pairs. The translation mode uses a square-bracketed free vector and the separate symbol $\oplus$. An ordered pair of points determines an anchored vector The ordered pair of points \[ (P,Q) \] determines a directed motion beginning at \(P\) and ending at \(Q\) . We represent it geometrically by the arrow \[ \overrightarrow{PQ} \] and call it an anchored vector . Its initial point is part of the object: changing \(P\) or \(Q\) produces a different anchored vector. An anchored vector Move $P$ and $Q$ to see the directed arrow determined by the ordered pair $(P,Q)$. ⛶ The anchored vector remembers both its initial point and its endpoint. Reversing the ordered pair reverses the motion. The coordinate change encoded by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is the ordinary point difference \[ \Delta(P,Q):=Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2). \] Its two entries answer two concrete questions: how much must the horizontal coordinate change? how much must the vertical coordinate change? Thus the anchored vector contains more information than \(\Delta(P,Q)\) : it records where the motion begins and ends , while \(\Delta(P,Q)\) records only the two coordinate changes. Its length is the distance between its endpoints: \[  oxed{|\overrightarrow{PQ}|=d_E(P,Q)}. \] Reversing the order negates the coordinate change: \[ \Delta(Q,P)=P-Q=-(Q-P). \] Different anchored vectors can encode the same displacement Now consider two anchored vectors \[ \overrightarrow{PQ}\qquad	ext{and}\qquad\overrightarrow{P'Q'}. \] If \((P,Q)
eq(P',Q')\) , then they are different anchored vectors. Nevertheless, they may encode the same coordinate change. This happens precisely when \[ \Delta(P,Q)=\Delta(P',Q'), \] or equivalently when \[ Q-P=Q'-P'. \] We therefore introduce the relation \[  oxed{ \overrightarrow{PQ}\sim\overrightarrow{P'Q'} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Delta(P,Q)=\Delta(P',Q'). } \] The symbol \(\sim\) does not say that the anchored vectors are literally the same ordered pair of points. It says that they encode the same displacement. Different anchored vectors with the same displacement The endpoint pairs differ, but the ordinary coordinate differences and the resulting free vectors agree. ⛶ Both arrows change the horizontal coordinate by $3$ and the vertical coordinate by $2$. Free vectors: extracting only the change of position Often we do not care where a motion begins. We want an instruction that can be applied at any point: change the horizontal coordinate by \(v_1\) and the vertical coordinate by \(v_2\) . To isolate exactly this information, we form a new abstract object. Free vector A free vector is the collection of all anchored vectors that encode one fixed coordinate change. If \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is one representative, then \[ [\overrightarrow{PQ}] :=\{\overrightarrow{AB}:\Delta(A,B)=\Delta(P,Q)\}. \] Every member of this collection is called a representative of the free vector. If \[ Q-P=(v_1,v_2), \] then the resulting free vector is written \[  oxed{\mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[v_1,v_2].} \] The round pair \((v_1,v_2)\) is the ordinary coordinate difference of two points. The square pair \([v_1,v_2]\) is the free vector carrying that displacement. This is the central abstraction: \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) says: “start at this particular point \(P\) and finish at this particular point \(Q\) ”; \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) says: “wherever you start, change your position by \(v_1\) horizontally and \(v_2\) vertically”. One free vector, many anchored representatives Move the endpoint $V$ of the representative beginning at $O=(0,0)$. ⛶ Every displayed arrow is a different anchored vector, but all are representatives of the same free vector. Turning a point difference into a free vector The square brackets now define the required change of type. Free vector determined by two points For points \(P\) and \(Q\) , define \[  oxed{ [Q-P] :=[\overrightarrow{PQ}] =[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2]. } \] The expression \(Q-P\) is the ordinary coordinate difference and is written with round brackets when expanded. The expression \([Q-P]\) is a free vector. In particular, \[ [Q-P]=-[P-Q]. \] This notation will later make expressions such as \[ \mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0] \] type-correct: both factors of the dot product are free vectors, so the result is a real number. Applying a free vector to a point A free vector is reusable because it can be anchored at any chosen point. We use a separate operation for this action. Point translated by a vector For \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2], \] define \[  oxed{ P\oplus\mathbf v :=(p_1+v_1,\,p_2+v_2). } \] The result is a point. Equivalently, the endpoint \(Q\) is characterised by \[  oxed{ Q=P\oplus\mathbf v \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad [Q-P]=\mathbf v. } \] Thus \[  oxed{P\oplus[Q-P]=Q}, \qquad  oxed{[(P\oplus\mathbf v)-P]=\mathbf v}. \] The order of the types matters. The expression \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) is defined, while \(\mathbf v\oplus P\) is not. This is an action of a displacement on a point, not a commutative addition of two objects of the same kind. The operations and their output types Let \(\mathcal P\) denote the set of Cartesian points and let \(V\) denote the set of free vectors. The operations used from now on have the following types: \[  egin{aligned} \mathcal P	imes\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (P,Q)&\longmapsto P+Q,\ \mathcal P	imes\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (Q,P)&\longmapsto Q-P,\ \mathbb R	imes\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (\lambda,P)&\longmapsto\lambda P,\[2mm] \mathcal P	imes\mathcal P&\longrightarrow V, & (Q,P)&\longmapsto[Q-P],\ \mathcal P	imes V&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (P,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto P\oplus\mathbf v,\[2mm] V	imes V&\longrightarrow V, & (\mathbf u,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto\mathbf u+\mathbf v,\ \mathbb R	imes V&\longrightarrow V, & (t,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto t\mathbf v,\ V	imes V&\longrightarrow\mathbb R, & (\mathbf u,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v. \end{aligned} \] The vector sum, scalar multiplication and dot product are developed in the following sections. The two bridge operations \[ [Q-P]\in V, \qquad P\oplus\mathbf v\in\mathcal P \] are what later make parametric and normal equations of lines and planes unambiguous. The length of a free vector All representatives of one free vector have the same horizontal and vertical coordinate changes. The distance formula therefore gives them the same length. Length is consequently a property of the free vector itself, not of the particular representative chosen to draw it. Euclidean norm Let \[ \mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]=[v_1,v_2]. \] The norm of \(\mathbf v\) is the length of any anchored representative: \[  oxed{ \|\mathbf v\| :=|\overrightarrow{PQ}| =d_E(P,Q) =\sqrt{(q_1-p_1)^2+(q_2-p_2)^2} =\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}. } \] The definition is independent of the representative: equivalent anchored vectors have the same coordinate change and therefore the same length. In particular, \[ \|\mathbf0\|=0, \qquad \mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\implies\|\mathbf v\|>0. \] For any two points \(A\) and \(B\) , \[  oxed{d_E(A,B)=\|[B-A]\|}. \] Thus a free vector carries two kinds of information: its components say how the position changes , and its norm says how far the motion travels . Similar notation, different objects This distinction must remain explicit: \[  egin{aligned} V&=(v_1,v_2) &&	ext{is a point},\ \overrightarrow{OV}& &&	ext{is an anchored vector},\ [V-O]&=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2] &&	ext{is a free vector}. \end{aligned} \] The expressions use the same numbers, but they do not denote the same kind of object. A representative beginning at the origin is especially convenient. If \[ O=(0,0),\qquad V=(v_1,v_2), \] then \[  oxed{\mathbf v=[V-O]=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=[v_1,v_2].} \] The point \(V\) is not identified with the free vector. It merely determines a canonical anchored representative from the origin. What the abstraction buys us A free vector is a reusable instruction for changing position. The operator \([Q-P]\) extracts such an instruction from two points, while \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) applies an instruction at a chosen point. Addition, opposite vectors, and subtraction Why free vectors should be addable A free vector is an instruction for changing position, independent of the point at which the motion begins. Once such instructions exist, the next natural problem is to compose them. Suppose that a first displacement is \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] and a second displacement is \[ \mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]. \] Starting at an arbitrary point \(P\) , apply \(\mathbf a\) and then \(\mathbf b\) . Using the point--vector operation introduced above, define \[ Q=P\oplus\mathbf a, \qquad R=Q\oplus\mathbf b. \] Equivalently, \[ [Q-P]=\mathbf a, \qquad [R-Q]=\mathbf b. \] The two-stage journey is \[ P\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow R, \] and the direct displacement from its initial point to its final point is the free vector \[ [R-P]. \] The composition problem Does \([R-P]\) depend only on \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) , or does it also depend on the chosen starting point \(P\) ? Two successive changes and one total change Move $P$, $Q$, or $R$ and compare $[Q-P]$, $[R-Q]$, and $[R-P]$. ⛶ The anchored arrows are different objects. Their square-bracketed point differences are the corresponding free vectors. The total displacement is independent of the starting point Write \[ P=(p_1,p_2). \] Since \(Q=P\oplus\mathbf a\) , \[ Q=(p_1+a_1,\,p_2+a_2). \] Applying \(\mathbf b\) at \(Q\) gives \[  egin{aligned} R &=Q\oplus\mathbf b\ &=(p_1+a_1+b_1,\,p_2+a_2+b_2). \end{aligned} \] The ordinary point difference is therefore \[ R-P=(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2), \] and the associated free vector is \[ [R-P]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. \] The coordinates of \(P\) have disappeared. Hence the total displacement depends only on \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) . Addition of free vectors The sum \(\mathbf a+\mathbf b\) is the free vector representing the total effect of first applying \(\mathbf a\) and then applying \(\mathbf b\) : \[  oxed{ \mathbf a+\mathbf b :=[R-P] =[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. } \] The definition is compatible with the action of vectors on points: \[  oxed{ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus\mathbf b =P\oplus(\mathbf a+\mathbf b). } \] For three points \(P,Q,R\) , this same compatibility can be written as the chain rule for displacements: \[  oxed{ [R-P]=[Q-P]+[R-Q]. } \] The order of two displacements Coordinatewise addition gives \[ [a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2] =[b_1+a_1,\,b_2+a_2]. \] Hence \[  oxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=\mathbf b+\mathbf a.} \] This commutativity concerns two free vectors. It does not turn \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) into a commutative operation: the expression \(\mathbf v\oplus P\) remains undefined. Geometrically, the two possible orders trace adjacent sides of a parallelogram and lead to the same final point: \[ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus\mathbf b = (P\oplus\mathbf b)\oplus\mathbf a. \] Opposite displacement For \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] the opposite free vector is the instruction that undoes its change of position: \[  oxed{-\mathbf a=[-a_1,-a_2].} \] Applying \(\mathbf a\) and then \(-\mathbf a\) returns to the initial point: \[ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus(-\mathbf a)=P. \] Therefore \[ \mathbf a+(-\mathbf a)=\mathbf0, \qquad \mathbf0=[0,0]. \] The opposite vector has the same norm as \(\mathbf a\) but the reverse orientation. Subtraction of free vectors Once the opposite vector has been defined, subtraction is addition of the opposite. Subtraction of free vectors For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(\mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]\) , \[  oxed{ \mathbf a-\mathbf b :=\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b) =[a_1-b_1,\,a_2-b_2]. } \] Consequently, \[ (\mathbf a-\mathbf b)+\mathbf b=\mathbf a. \] Do not confuse the two subtractions The ordinary point difference \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2) \] is a round coordinate pair. The free vector from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \[  oxed{[Q-P]=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2].} \] Vector subtraction \(\mathbf a-\mathbf b\) is a separate operation on two free vectors. Scaling vectors Multiplication by a real number A free vector is an instruction for changing position. Multiplying it by a real number changes the size of that displacement and, when the number is negative, reverses its orientation. Scalar multiplication For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(t\in\mathbb R\) , \[  oxed{t\mathbf a=[ta_1,ta_2]}. \] If \(t>0\) , the displacement keeps its orientation and its length is multiplied by \(t\) : \[ \|t\mathbf a\|=t\|\mathbf a\|. \] If \(t<0\) , the displacement points in the opposite orientation and \[ \|t\mathbf a\|=|t|\,\|\mathbf a\|. \] For \(t=0\) , every vector is sent to the zero vector \(\mathbf0=[0,0]\) . Scaling one vector $2\mathbf u$ ⛶ $t$ = 2 The dashed arrow is $\mathbf u$; the solid arrow is $t\mathbf u$. Scalar multiplication is compatible with addition: \[  oxed{t(\mathbf a+\mathbf b)=t\mathbf a+t\mathbf b}, \qquad  oxed{(s+t)\mathbf a=s\mathbf a+t\mathbf a}. \] These identities follow directly from coordinatewise arithmetic. How much motion and in which direction? The norm introduced with free vectors already answers the first geometric question: it tells us how long the displacement is. We now combine that length with the direction in which the vector points. Two questions carried by every nonzero vector How long is the displacement? In which direction does it point? Let \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) be drawn from the origin. Its endpoint, together with its horizontal and vertical coordinate changes, forms a right triangle. The legs have lengths \(|v_1|\) and \(|v_2|\) , while the representative of \(\mathbf v\) is the hypotenuse. A vector as a right triangle $\mathbf v=[4,3]$ ⛶ The vector, its two coordinate changes, its norm and its inclination are displayed simultaneously. The distance formula used in the definition of the norm gives \[  oxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] The point of the present section is not to define this length again, but to show how the same right triangle separates the vector into a magnitude and a direction. Separating magnitude from direction Assume \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) and let \(\alpha\) be its angle of inclination measured from the positive \(x\) -axis. Recovering the coordinates The right triangle gives \[ \cos\alpha= rac{v_1}{\|\mathbf v\|}, \qquad \sin\alpha= rac{v_2}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Therefore \[ v_1=\|\mathbf v\|\cos\alpha, \qquad v_2=\|\mathbf v\|\sin\alpha. \] Magnitude-direction decomposition Every nonzero vector admits the decomposition \[  oxed{\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]}. \] The vector \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) has length one. The two pieces of a vector \(\|\mathbf v\|\) says how much displacement occurs; \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) says in which direction it occurs. Thus every nonzero vector is a positive magnitude multiplied by a unit direction. Parallelism, perpendicularity, and the dot product The preceding geometry of the unit circle already tells us how to study the angle between two unit directions. Every nonzero vector can be rescaled to such a unit direction: \[ \widehat{\mathbf u}:= rac{\mathbf u}{\|\mathbf u\|}, \qquad \widehat{\mathbf v}:= rac{\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Both normalized vectors have length \(1\) , and positive rescaling does not change their directions. Therefore the angle between \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) is exactly the angle between \(\widehat{\mathbf u}\) and \(\widehat{\mathbf v}\) . The unit-circle formulas from the previous lecture can consequently be transferred directly to arbitrary nonzero vectors; the factors \(\|\mathbf u\|\) and \(\|\mathbf v\|\) merely restore their original lengths. Take two arbitrary nonzero vectors \[ \mathbf u=[u_1,u_2],\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]. \] Let their angles of inclination be \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) . Then \[ \mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha], \qquad \mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos eta,\sin eta]. \] One relative angle controls both tests The directed turn from \(\mathbf u\) to \(\mathbf v\) is \( eta-\alpha\) . Its sine detects parallel directions, while its cosine measures how strongly the directions agree. Two vectors, two coordinate tests Move the endpoints of $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The sine and cosine of the same relative angle produce the determinant and dot-product tests. Parallel directions arise from the sine Parallelism from \(\sin( eta-\alpha)\) Two nonzero vectors determine the same unoriented direction precisely when \[  eta-\alpha\equiv0\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\sin( eta-\alpha)=0\) . Using \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)=\sin eta\cos\alpha-\cos eta\sin\alpha \] and substituting the coordinate expressions gives \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_2-u_2v_1}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The denominator is nonzero, so the sine vanishes exactly when the numerator vanishes. Parallelism test \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0}. \] Equivalently, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v	ext{ for some }\lambda
eq0}. \] Why the determinant condition gives a scalar multiple If \(v_1
eq0\) , set \(\lambda=u_1/v_1\) . The equality \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) then gives \(u_2=\lambda v_2\) , hence \(\mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v\) . If \(v_1=0\) , then \(v_2
eq0\) and the same argument uses \(\lambda=u_2/v_2\) . The quantity \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1\) measures whether the two directions span genuine area. It is zero exactly when both vectors lie on one line. The cosine produces the dot product From \(\cos( eta-\alpha)\) to a coordinate operation The identity \[ \cos( eta-\alpha)=\cos eta\cos\alpha+\sin eta\sin\alpha \] gives \[ \cos( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_1+u_2v_2}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The numerator is therefore the coordinate expression that records the cosine of the relative angle. Dot product For vectors in the plane, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=u_1v_1+u_2v_2}. \] In three-dimensional space, \[  oxed{[u_1,u_2,u_3]\cdot[v_1,v_2,v_3]=u_1v_1+u_2v_2+u_3v_3}. \] What the dot product tells us If \(	heta\in[0,\pi]\) is the ordinary angle between two nonzero vectors, then \[  oxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos	heta}. \] Hence \[  oxed{\mathbf u\perp\mathbf v\iff\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0}, \] and, when the numerical value of the angle is needed, \[  oxed{	heta=\arccos\!\left( rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}ight)}. \] Because the quotient was obtained as a cosine, it already lies in \([-1,1]\) . The dot product is positive for an acute angle, zero for a right angle, and negative for an obtuse angle. Example For \(\mathbf u=[3,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-2,3]\) , \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=3(-2)+2(3)=0, \] so the vectors are perpendicular. The cosine theorem The displacement between the endpoints of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) is \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) . Expanding its coordinate length gives \[  egin{aligned} \|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2 &=(u_1-v_1)^2+(u_2-v_2)^2\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v)\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos	heta. \end{aligned} \] Therefore, for a triangle with side lengths \(a\) , \(b\) , and \(c\) opposite the included angle \(	heta\) , \[  oxed{c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos	heta}. \] Orthogonal projection Let \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) . We want to split \(\mathbf u\) into a displacement parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and a perpendicular remainder. A vector parallel to \(\mathbf v\) has the form \(\lambda\mathbf v\) , so we seek \(\lambda\) such that \[ \mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v\perp\mathbf v. \] Orthogonal projection Move $\mathbf u$ and the direction $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The projection is the multiple of $\mathbf v$ whose remainder from $\mathbf u$ is perpendicular to $\mathbf v$. The right-angle condition determines the coefficient Write \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) . Perpendicularity requires \[ (\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf v=0. \] In coordinates this is \[ (u_1-\lambda v_1)v_1+(u_2-\lambda v_2)v_2=0, \] hence \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\lambda(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v)=0. \] Since \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) , we have \(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|^2>0\) , so \[ \lambda= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}. \] Projection formula For \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) , \[  oxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u = rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\,\mathbf v}. \] This is not an independent rule: it is the unique multiple of \(\mathbf v\) that leaves a perpendicular remainder. Orthogonal decomposition Every vector \(\mathbf u\) can be written as \[  oxed{ \mathbf u =\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u +\left(\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight) }. \] The first term is parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and the second is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Therefore the Pythagorean theorem gives \[  oxed{ \|\mathbf u\|^2 =\left\|\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2 +\left\|\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2 }. \] Linear combinations and change of basis Linear combinations of two vectors Choose two vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . We want to describe the displacements obtained by assigning an independent coefficient to each direction and then adding the two resulting motions. Linear combination Given vectors \(\mathbf u,\mathbf v\) and real numbers \(a,b\) , the vector \[  oxed{a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v} \] is called a linear combination of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . The coefficient \(a\) controls the contribution of \(\mathbf u\) , while \(b\) controls the contribution of \(\mathbf v\) . A linear combination of two vectors $2\mathbf u-\mathbf v$ ⛶ $a$ = 2 $b$ = -1 The two contributions $a\mathbf u$ and $b\mathbf v$ are placed head to tail; the dark arrow is their sum. One direction versus two directions A single nonzero vector generates only the line \[ \{\lambda\mathbf v:\lambda\in\mathbb R\}. \] Two vectors lying on the same line still generate only that line. Two vectors with different directions provide two independent motions and can generate the whole plane. Why two independent directions reach the plane If \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) lie on one line, every sum \(a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v\) remains on that line. If their directions differ, the parallelogram construction decomposes any target displacement into one component along each direction. The coefficients record those two signed motions. Basis theorem in the plane Two vectors with different directions determine every vector in the plane uniquely in the form \[  oxed{\mathbf w=a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v}. \] A basis is precisely such a pair of independent directions, and basis coordinates are the coefficients in this reconstruction. One vector in a different basis A vector does not change when we describe it in another basis. Only the numbers used to reconstruct it from the chosen basis vectors change. One numerical example is enough to see the mechanism. Let \[ \mathbf b_1=[1,1],\qquad \mathbf b_2=[1,-1] \] and consider \[ \mathbf v=[4,2]. \] We seek numbers \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) such that \[ \mathbf v=\alpha\mathbf b_1+ eta\mathbf b_2. \] The same vector, two numerical descriptions $\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2$ ⛶ $\alpha$ = 3 $ eta$ = 1 The sliders show how the same vector is reconstructed from the two chosen basis directions. Solving for the basis coordinates Using the Cartesian coordinates of the basis vectors gives \[  egin{aligned} [4,2] &=\alpha[1,1]+ eta[1,-1]\ &=[\alpha+ eta,\alpha- eta]. \end{aligned} \] Therefore \[ \alpha+ eta=4, \qquad \alpha- eta=2. \] Solving this small system gives \[ \alpha=3, \qquad  eta=1. \] Hence \[  oxed{\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2} \] and the coordinates of the same vector in the basis \(B=(\mathbf b_1,\mathbf b_2)\) are \[  oxed{[\mathbf v]_B=[3,1]}. \] What changes and what does not The Cartesian coordinates \([4,2]\) and the basis coordinates \([3,1]_B\) are two numerical descriptions of the same geometric vector. Changing the basis means solving for the coefficients in one concrete linear combination; no additional general machinery is introduced here. Exercises The first ten consolidate calculation and construction. The next ten require explanation, proof, comparison, or modelling. Download exercises as Markdown A. Elementary computational and graphical exercises Let \(A=(1,2)\) and \(B=(5,-1)\) . Compute the ordinary point difference \(B-A\) and the free vector \([B-A]=[\overrightarrow{AB}]\) . Find the points \(O\oplus[B-A]\) for \(O=(0,0)\) and \(P\oplus[B-A]\) for \(P=(-2,3)\) , and draw the corresponding anchored representatives. For \(\mathbf u=[3,-2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,4]\) , compute \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , \(2\mathbf u\) , and \(-3\mathbf v\) . Draw the parallelogram for \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) . For \(\mathbf u=[1,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[2,-1]\) , compute \(2\mathbf u-3\mathbf v\) and reproduce it head to tail. Compute the lengths and unit vectors for \([3,4]\) , \([-5,12]\) , and \([2,-2]\) . Classify the pairs \([2,1],[6,3]\) ; \([2,1],[1,-2]\) ; \([3,-1],[2,5]\) as parallel, perpendicular, or neither. Find the angles between \([1,0]\) and \([1,1]\) , and between \([1,1]\) and \([-1,1]\) . For \(\mathbf u=[3,1]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,2]\) , compute \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) directly and from the cosine theorem. Project \([4,3]\) onto \([1,0]\) and onto \([1,1]\) . Verify that each remainder is perpendicular to the target direction. For \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) , and \(\mathbf u=[4,2]\) , find \([\mathbf u]_B\) and reconstruct \(\mathbf u\) . Starting from \(P=(2,-1)\) , form \((P\oplus[3,2])\oplus[-1,4]\) and then apply \(-2[3,2]\) . Find the endpoint. Combine the three free vectors first and verify that one translation \(P\oplus igl([3,2]+[-1,4]-2[3,2] igr)\) gives the same point. B. Development and reasoning exercises Explain with a parallelogram why translating an arrow without changing its directed change does not change the free vector. Give a numerical example. Draw \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) for concrete vectors and explain geometrically why the endpoints coincide. Then verify that \((P\oplus\mathbf u)\oplus\mathbf v=P\oplus(\mathbf u+\mathbf v)\) . Prove that two nonzero plane vectors are parallel exactly when one is a scalar multiple of the other. Discuss positive and negative multiples separately. Find equal-length vectors with \(\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|<\|\mathbf u\|\) and explain the cancellation geometrically. Starting from the coordinate definition of the dot product, prove distributivity, homogeneity, symmetry, and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|^2\) . Use these properties to expand \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2\) and recover the cosine theorem. Let \(\mathbf r=\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\) for \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) . Prove that \(\mathbf r\perp\mathbf v\) , then use \(\|\mathbf r\|^2\geq0\) to derive the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality \(|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|\leq\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\) . Determine when equality holds. Explain why two nonparallel basis vectors give unique coefficients in \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+ eta\mathbf b_2\) . Explain the type and meaning of each expression: \(P+Q\) , \(Q-P\) , \([Q-P]\) , \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v\) . State which expressions produce a point, a free vector, or a real number. Find the standard and \(B\) -coordinates of \([6,2]\) for \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) . Explain when a basis is well adapted. Refute the claim that every pair of nonzero vectors is a basis. Give one dependent and one independent example and interpret their linear combinations.
docs.title: Lecture 02 · Vectors, Bases, and Coordinate Systems

docs.text: A Cartesian point is an ordered pair of real numbers. Let \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad Q=(q_1,q_2). \] The notation is deliberately type-sensitive: \((p_1,p_2)\) denotes a point ; \((P,Q)\) denotes an ordered pair of points ; \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) denotes the anchored vector determined by that ordered pair; \([v_1,v_2]\) denotes a free vector , an abstract displacement independent of its starting point; \([Q-P]\) denotes the free vector extracted from the ordinary coordinate difference of the two points; \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) denotes the point obtained by applying the free vector \(\mathbf v\) at \(P\) . Round and square brackets therefore carry different information. Round brackets describe points. Square brackets describe free vectors.
docs.title: From Cartesian points to free vectors

docs.text: Because Cartesian points are represented by ordered pairs of numbers, their coordinate descriptions admit the usual coordinatewise arithmetic. Write \[ O=(0,0). \] Then \[ \begin{aligned} P+Q&=(p_1+q_1,\,p_2+q_2),\\ -P&=(-p_1,-p_2),\\ Q-P&=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2),\\ \lambda P&=(\lambda p_1,\lambda p_2). \end{aligned} \] These are operations on point coordinates, and their results are again written with round brackets. In particular, \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2) \] is still an ordinary coordinate pair. It records the two numerical changes needed to move from \(P\) to \(Q\) , but it has not yet been declared to be a free vector. Arithmetic on Cartesian points Compare coordinate arithmetic with the separate operation of translating a point by a vector. ⛶ Point addition Opposite point Point subtraction Scale point coordinates Point $\oplus$ vector $\lambda$ = 1.5 The ordinary operations use round coordinate pairs. The translation mode uses a square-bracketed free vector and the separate symbol $\oplus$.
docs.title: Arithmetic on Cartesian point coordinates

docs.text: The ordered pair of points \[ (P,Q) \] determines a directed motion beginning at \(P\) and ending at \(Q\) . We represent it geometrically by the arrow \[ \overrightarrow{PQ} \] and call it an anchored vector . Its initial point is part of the object: changing \(P\) or \(Q\) produces a different anchored vector. An anchored vector Move $P$ and $Q$ to see the directed arrow determined by the ordered pair $(P,Q)$. ⛶ The anchored vector remembers both its initial point and its endpoint. Reversing the ordered pair reverses the motion. The coordinate change encoded by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is the ordinary point difference \[ \Delta(P,Q):=Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2). \] Its two entries answer two concrete questions: how much must the horizontal coordinate change? how much must the vertical coordinate change? Thus the anchored vector contains more information than \(\Delta(P,Q)\) : it records where the motion begins and ends , while \(\Delta(P,Q)\) records only the two coordinate changes. Its length is the distance between its endpoints: \[ \boxed{|\overrightarrow{PQ}|=d_E(P,Q)}. \] Reversing the order negates the coordinate change: \[ \Delta(Q,P)=P-Q=-(Q-P). \]
docs.title: An ordered pair of points determines an anchored vector

docs.text: Now consider two anchored vectors \[ \overrightarrow{PQ}\qquad\text{and}\qquad\overrightarrow{P'Q'}. \] If \((P,Q)\neq(P',Q')\) , then they are different anchored vectors. Nevertheless, they may encode the same coordinate change. This happens precisely when \[ \Delta(P,Q)=\Delta(P',Q'), \] or equivalently when \[ Q-P=Q'-P'. \] We therefore introduce the relation \[ \boxed{ \overrightarrow{PQ}\sim\overrightarrow{P'Q'} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Delta(P,Q)=\Delta(P',Q'). } \] The symbol \(\sim\) does not say that the anchored vectors are literally the same ordered pair of points. It says that they encode the same displacement. Different anchored vectors with the same displacement The endpoint pairs differ, but the ordinary coordinate differences and the resulting free vectors agree. ⛶ Both arrows change the horizontal coordinate by $3$ and the vertical coordinate by $2$.
docs.title: Different anchored vectors can encode the same displacement

docs.text: Often we do not care where a motion begins. We want an instruction that can be applied at any point: change the horizontal coordinate by \(v_1\) and the vertical coordinate by \(v_2\) . To isolate exactly this information, we form a new abstract object. Free vector A free vector is the collection of all anchored vectors that encode one fixed coordinate change. If \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is one representative, then \[ [\overrightarrow{PQ}] :=\{\overrightarrow{AB}:\Delta(A,B)=\Delta(P,Q)\}. \] Every member of this collection is called a representative of the free vector. If \[ Q-P=(v_1,v_2), \] then the resulting free vector is written \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[v_1,v_2].} \] The round pair \((v_1,v_2)\) is the ordinary coordinate difference of two points. The square pair \([v_1,v_2]\) is the free vector carrying that displacement. This is the central abstraction: \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) says: “start at this particular point \(P\) and finish at this particular point \(Q\) ”; \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) says: “wherever you start, change your position by \(v_1\) horizontally and \(v_2\) vertically”. One free vector, many anchored representatives Move the endpoint $V$ of the representative beginning at $O=(0,0)$. ⛶ Every displayed arrow is a different anchored vector, but all are representatives of the same free vector.
docs.title: Free vectors: extracting only the change of position

docs.text: The square brackets now define the required change of type. Free vector determined by two points For points \(P\) and \(Q\) , define \[ \boxed{ [Q-P] :=[\overrightarrow{PQ}] =[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2]. } \] The expression \(Q-P\) is the ordinary coordinate difference and is written with round brackets when expanded. The expression \([Q-P]\) is a free vector. In particular, \[ [Q-P]=-[P-Q]. \] This notation will later make expressions such as \[ \mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0] \] type-correct: both factors of the dot product are free vectors, so the result is a real number.
docs.title: Turning a point difference into a free vector

docs.text: A free vector is reusable because it can be anchored at any chosen point. We use a separate operation for this action. Point translated by a vector For \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2], \] define \[ \boxed{ P\oplus\mathbf v :=(p_1+v_1,\,p_2+v_2). } \] The result is a point. Equivalently, the endpoint \(Q\) is characterised by \[ \boxed{ Q=P\oplus\mathbf v \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad [Q-P]=\mathbf v. } \] Thus \[ \boxed{P\oplus[Q-P]=Q}, \qquad \boxed{[(P\oplus\mathbf v)-P]=\mathbf v}. \] The order of the types matters. The expression \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) is defined, while \(\mathbf v\oplus P\) is not. This is an action of a displacement on a point, not a commutative addition of two objects of the same kind.
docs.title: Applying a free vector to a point

docs.text: Let \(\mathcal P\) denote the set of Cartesian points and let \(V\) denote the set of free vectors. The operations used from now on have the following types: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathcal P\times\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (P,Q)&\longmapsto P+Q,\\ \mathcal P\times\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (Q,P)&\longmapsto Q-P,\\ \mathbb R\times\mathcal P&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (\lambda,P)&\longmapsto\lambda P,\\[2mm] \mathcal P\times\mathcal P&\longrightarrow V, & (Q,P)&\longmapsto[Q-P],\\ \mathcal P\times V&\longrightarrow\mathcal P, & (P,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto P\oplus\mathbf v,\\[2mm] V\times V&\longrightarrow V, & (\mathbf u,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto\mathbf u+\mathbf v,\\ \mathbb R\times V&\longrightarrow V, & (t,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto t\mathbf v,\\ V\times V&\longrightarrow\mathbb R, & (\mathbf u,\mathbf v)&\longmapsto\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v. \end{aligned} \] The vector sum, scalar multiplication and dot product are developed in the following sections. The two bridge operations \[ [Q-P]\in V, \qquad P\oplus\mathbf v\in\mathcal P \] are what later make parametric and normal equations of lines and planes unambiguous.
docs.title: The operations and their output types

docs.text: All representatives of one free vector have the same horizontal and vertical coordinate changes. The distance formula therefore gives them the same length. Length is consequently a property of the free vector itself, not of the particular representative chosen to draw it. Euclidean norm Let \[ \mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]=[v_1,v_2]. \] The norm of \(\mathbf v\) is the length of any anchored representative: \[ \boxed{ \|\mathbf v\| :=|\overrightarrow{PQ}| =d_E(P,Q) =\sqrt{(q_1-p_1)^2+(q_2-p_2)^2} =\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}. } \] The definition is independent of the representative: equivalent anchored vectors have the same coordinate change and therefore the same length. In particular, \[ \|\mathbf0\|=0, \qquad \mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\implies\|\mathbf v\|>0. \] For any two points \(A\) and \(B\) , \[ \boxed{d_E(A,B)=\|[B-A]\|}. \] Thus a free vector carries two kinds of information: its components say how the position changes , and its norm says how far the motion travels .
docs.title: The length of a free vector

docs.text: This distinction must remain explicit: \[ \begin{aligned} V&=(v_1,v_2) &&\text{is a point},\\ \overrightarrow{OV}& &&\text{is an anchored vector},\\ [V-O]&=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2] &&\text{is a free vector}. \end{aligned} \] The expressions use the same numbers, but they do not denote the same kind of object. A representative beginning at the origin is especially convenient. If \[ O=(0,0),\qquad V=(v_1,v_2), \] then \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=[V-O]=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=[v_1,v_2].} \] The point \(V\) is not identified with the free vector. It merely determines a canonical anchored representative from the origin. What the abstraction buys us A free vector is a reusable instruction for changing position. The operator \([Q-P]\) extracts such an instruction from two points, while \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) applies an instruction at a chosen point.
docs.title: Similar notation, different objects

docs.text: A free vector is an instruction for changing position, independent of the point at which the motion begins. Once such instructions exist, the next natural problem is to compose them. Suppose that a first displacement is \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] and a second displacement is \[ \mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]. \] Starting at an arbitrary point \(P\) , apply \(\mathbf a\) and then \(\mathbf b\) . Using the point--vector operation introduced above, define \[ Q=P\oplus\mathbf a, \qquad R=Q\oplus\mathbf b. \] Equivalently, \[ [Q-P]=\mathbf a, \qquad [R-Q]=\mathbf b. \] The two-stage journey is \[ P\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow R, \] and the direct displacement from its initial point to its final point is the free vector \[ [R-P]. \] The composition problem Does \([R-P]\) depend only on \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) , or does it also depend on the chosen starting point \(P\) ? Two successive changes and one total change Move $P$, $Q$, or $R$ and compare $[Q-P]$, $[R-Q]$, and $[R-P]$. ⛶ The anchored arrows are different objects. Their square-bracketed point differences are the corresponding free vectors.
docs.title: Why free vectors should be addable

docs.text: Write \[ P=(p_1,p_2). \] Since \(Q=P\oplus\mathbf a\) , \[ Q=(p_1+a_1,\,p_2+a_2). \] Applying \(\mathbf b\) at \(Q\) gives \[ \begin{aligned} R &=Q\oplus\mathbf b\\ &=(p_1+a_1+b_1,\,p_2+a_2+b_2). \end{aligned} \] The ordinary point difference is therefore \[ R-P=(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2), \] and the associated free vector is \[ [R-P]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. \] The coordinates of \(P\) have disappeared. Hence the total displacement depends only on \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) . Addition of free vectors The sum \(\mathbf a+\mathbf b\) is the free vector representing the total effect of first applying \(\mathbf a\) and then applying \(\mathbf b\) : \[ \boxed{ \mathbf a+\mathbf b :=[R-P] =[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. } \] The definition is compatible with the action of vectors on points: \[ \boxed{ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus\mathbf b =P\oplus(\mathbf a+\mathbf b). } \] For three points \(P,Q,R\) , this same compatibility can be written as the chain rule for displacements: \[ \boxed{ [R-P]=[Q-P]+[R-Q]. } \]
docs.title: The total displacement is independent of the starting point

docs.text: Coordinatewise addition gives \[ [a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2] =[b_1+a_1,\,b_2+a_2]. \] Hence \[ \boxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=\mathbf b+\mathbf a.} \] This commutativity concerns two free vectors. It does not turn \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) into a commutative operation: the expression \(\mathbf v\oplus P\) remains undefined. Geometrically, the two possible orders trace adjacent sides of a parallelogram and lead to the same final point: \[ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus\mathbf b = (P\oplus\mathbf b)\oplus\mathbf a. \]
docs.title: The order of two displacements

docs.text: For \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] the opposite free vector is the instruction that undoes its change of position: \[ \boxed{-\mathbf a=[-a_1,-a_2].} \] Applying \(\mathbf a\) and then \(-\mathbf a\) returns to the initial point: \[ (P\oplus\mathbf a)\oplus(-\mathbf a)=P. \] Therefore \[ \mathbf a+(-\mathbf a)=\mathbf0, \qquad \mathbf0=[0,0]. \] The opposite vector has the same norm as \(\mathbf a\) but the reverse orientation.
docs.title: Opposite displacement

docs.text: Once the opposite vector has been defined, subtraction is addition of the opposite. Subtraction of free vectors For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(\mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]\) , \[ \boxed{ \mathbf a-\mathbf b :=\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b) =[a_1-b_1,\,a_2-b_2]. } \] Consequently, \[ (\mathbf a-\mathbf b)+\mathbf b=\mathbf a. \] Do not confuse the two subtractions The ordinary point difference \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2) \] is a round coordinate pair. The free vector from \(P\) to \(Q\) is \[ \boxed{[Q-P]=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2].} \] Vector subtraction \(\mathbf a-\mathbf b\) is a separate operation on two free vectors.
docs.title: Subtraction of free vectors

docs.text: A free vector is an instruction for changing position. Multiplying it by a real number changes the size of that displacement and, when the number is negative, reverses its orientation. Scalar multiplication For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(t\in\mathbb R\) , \[ \boxed{t\mathbf a=[ta_1,ta_2]}. \] If \(t>0\) , the displacement keeps its orientation and its length is multiplied by \(t\) : \[ \|t\mathbf a\|=t\|\mathbf a\|. \] If \(t<0\) , the displacement points in the opposite orientation and \[ \|t\mathbf a\|=|t|\,\|\mathbf a\|. \] For \(t=0\) , every vector is sent to the zero vector \(\mathbf0=[0,0]\) . Scaling one vector $2\mathbf u$ ⛶ $t$ = 2 The dashed arrow is $\mathbf u$; the solid arrow is $t\mathbf u$. Scalar multiplication is compatible with addition: \[ \boxed{t(\mathbf a+\mathbf b)=t\mathbf a+t\mathbf b}, \qquad \boxed{(s+t)\mathbf a=s\mathbf a+t\mathbf a}. \] These identities follow directly from coordinatewise arithmetic.
docs.title: Multiplication by a real number

docs.text: The norm introduced with free vectors already answers the first geometric question: it tells us how long the displacement is. We now combine that length with the direction in which the vector points. Two questions carried by every nonzero vector How long is the displacement? In which direction does it point? Let \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) be drawn from the origin. Its endpoint, together with its horizontal and vertical coordinate changes, forms a right triangle. The legs have lengths \(|v_1|\) and \(|v_2|\) , while the representative of \(\mathbf v\) is the hypotenuse. A vector as a right triangle $\mathbf v=[4,3]$ ⛶ The vector, its two coordinate changes, its norm and its inclination are displayed simultaneously. The distance formula used in the definition of the norm gives \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] The point of the present section is not to define this length again, but to show how the same right triangle separates the vector into a magnitude and a direction.
docs.title: How much motion and in which direction?

docs.text: Assume \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) and let \(\alpha\) be its angle of inclination measured from the positive \(x\) -axis. Recovering the coordinates The right triangle gives \[ \cos\alpha=\frac{v_1}{\|\mathbf v\|}, \qquad \sin\alpha=\frac{v_2}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Therefore \[ v_1=\|\mathbf v\|\cos\alpha, \qquad v_2=\|\mathbf v\|\sin\alpha. \] Magnitude-direction decomposition Every nonzero vector admits the decomposition \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]}. \] The vector \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) has length one. The two pieces of a vector \(\|\mathbf v\|\) says how much displacement occurs; \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) says in which direction it occurs. Thus every nonzero vector is a positive magnitude multiplied by a unit direction.
docs.title: Separating magnitude from direction

docs.text: The preceding geometry of the unit circle already tells us how to study the angle between two unit directions. Every nonzero vector can be rescaled to such a unit direction: \[ \widehat{\mathbf u}:=\frac{\mathbf u}{\|\mathbf u\|}, \qquad \widehat{\mathbf v}:=\frac{\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Both normalized vectors have length \(1\) , and positive rescaling does not change their directions. Therefore the angle between \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) is exactly the angle between \(\widehat{\mathbf u}\) and \(\widehat{\mathbf v}\) . The unit-circle formulas from the previous lecture can consequently be transferred directly to arbitrary nonzero vectors; the factors \(\|\mathbf u\|\) and \(\|\mathbf v\|\) merely restore their original lengths. Take two arbitrary nonzero vectors \[ \mathbf u=[u_1,u_2],\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]. \] Let their angles of inclination be \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) . Then \[ \mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha], \qquad \mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\beta,\sin\beta]. \] One relative angle controls both tests The directed turn from \(\mathbf u\) to \(\mathbf v\) is \(\beta-\alpha\) . Its sine detects parallel directions, while its cosine measures how strongly the directions agree. Two vectors, two coordinate tests Move the endpoints of $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The sine and cosine of the same relative angle produce the determinant and dot-product tests.
docs.title: Parallelism, perpendicularity, and the dot product

docs.text:
Parallelism from \(\sin( eta-\alpha)\) Two nonzero vectors determine the same unoriented direction precisely when \[  eta-\alpha\equiv0\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\sin( eta-\alpha)=0\) . Using \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)=\sin eta\cos\alpha-\cos eta\sin\alpha \] and substituting the coordinate expressions gives \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_2-u_2v_1}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The denominator is nonzero, so the sine vanishes exactly when the numerator vanishes. Parallelism test \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0}. \] Equivalently, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v	ext{ for some }\lambda
eq0}. \] Why the determinant condition gives a scalar multiple If \(v_1
eq0\) , set \(\lambda=u_1/v_1\) . The equality \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) then gives \(u_2=\lambda v_2\) , hence \(\mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v\) . If \(v_1=0\) , then \(v_2
eq0\) and the same argument uses \(\lambda=u_2/v_2\) . The quantity \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1\) measures whether the two directions span genuine area. It is zero exactly when both vectors lie on one line.
docs.title: Parallel directions arise from the sine

docs.text: From \(\cos(\beta-\alpha)\) to a coordinate operation The identity \[ \cos(\beta-\alpha)=\cos\beta\cos\alpha+\sin\beta\sin\alpha \] gives \[ \cos(\beta-\alpha)=\frac{u_1v_1+u_2v_2}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The numerator is therefore the coordinate expression that records the cosine of the relative angle. Dot product For vectors in the plane, \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=u_1v_1+u_2v_2}. \] In three-dimensional space, \[ \boxed{[u_1,u_2,u_3]\cdot[v_1,v_2,v_3]=u_1v_1+u_2v_2+u_3v_3}. \] What the dot product tells us If \(\theta\in[0,\pi]\) is the ordinary angle between two nonzero vectors, then \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos\theta}. \] Hence \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\perp\mathbf v\iff\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0}, \] and, when the numerical value of the angle is needed, \[ \boxed{\theta=\arccos\!\left(\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}\right)}. \] Because the quotient was obtained as a cosine, it already lies in \([-1,1]\) . The dot product is positive for an acute angle, zero for a right angle, and negative for an obtuse angle. Example For \(\mathbf u=[3,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-2,3]\) , \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=3(-2)+2(3)=0, \] so the vectors are perpendicular.
docs.title: The cosine produces the dot product

docs.text: The displacement between the endpoints of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) is \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) . Expanding its coordinate length gives \[ \begin{aligned} \|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2 &=(u_1-v_1)^2+(u_2-v_2)^2\\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v)\\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos\theta. \end{aligned} \] Therefore, for a triangle with side lengths \(a\) , \(b\) , and \(c\) opposite the included angle \(\theta\) , \[ \boxed{c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos\theta}. \]
docs.title: The cosine theorem

docs.text:
Let \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) . We want to split \(\mathbf u\) into a displacement parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and a perpendicular remainder. A vector parallel to \(\mathbf v\) has the form \(\lambda\mathbf v\) , so we seek \(\lambda\) such that \[ \mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v\perp\mathbf v. \] Orthogonal projection Move $\mathbf u$ and the direction $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The projection is the multiple of $\mathbf v$ whose remainder from $\mathbf u$ is perpendicular to $\mathbf v$. The right-angle condition determines the coefficient Write \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) . Perpendicularity requires \[ (\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf v=0. \] In coordinates this is \[ (u_1-\lambda v_1)v_1+(u_2-\lambda v_2)v_2=0, \] hence \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\lambda(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v)=0. \] Since \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) , we have \(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|^2>0\) , so \[ \lambda= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}. \] Projection formula For \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) , \[  oxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u = rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\,\mathbf v}. \] This is not an independent rule: it is the unique multiple of \(\mathbf v\) that leaves a perpendicular remainder. Orthogonal decomposition Every vector \(\mathbf u\) can be written as \[  oxed{ \mathbf u =\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u +\left(\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight) }. \] The first term is parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and the second is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Therefore the Pythagorean theorem gives \[  oxed{ \|\mathbf u\|^2 =\left\|\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2 +\left\|\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2 }. \]
docs.title: Orthogonal projection
Linear combinations and change of basis

docs.text: Choose two vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . We want to describe the displacements obtained by assigning an independent coefficient to each direction and then adding the two resulting motions. Linear combination Given vectors \(\mathbf u,\mathbf v\) and real numbers \(a,b\) , the vector \[ \boxed{a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v} \] is called a linear combination of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . The coefficient \(a\) controls the contribution of \(\mathbf u\) , while \(b\) controls the contribution of \(\mathbf v\) . A linear combination of two vectors $2\mathbf u-\mathbf v$ ⛶ $a$ = 2 $b$ = -1 The two contributions $a\mathbf u$ and $b\mathbf v$ are placed head to tail; the dark arrow is their sum. One direction versus two directions A single nonzero vector generates only the line \[ \{\lambda\mathbf v:\lambda\in\mathbb R\}. \] Two vectors lying on the same line still generate only that line. Two vectors with different directions provide two independent motions and can generate the whole plane. Why two independent directions reach the plane If \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) lie on one line, every sum \(a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v\) remains on that line. If their directions differ, the parallelogram construction decomposes any target displacement into one component along each direction. The coefficients record those two signed motions. Basis theorem in the plane Two vectors with different directions determine every vector in the plane uniquely in the form \[ \boxed{\mathbf w=a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v}. \] A basis is precisely such a pair of independent directions, and basis coordinates are the coefficients in this reconstruction.
docs.title: Linear combinations of two vectors

docs.text: A vector does not change when we describe it in another basis. Only the numbers used to reconstruct it from the chosen basis vectors change. One numerical example is enough to see the mechanism. Let \[ \mathbf b_1=[1,1],\qquad \mathbf b_2=[1,-1] \] and consider \[ \mathbf v=[4,2]. \] We seek numbers \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) such that \[ \mathbf v=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2. \] The same vector, two numerical descriptions $\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2$ ⛶ $\alpha$ = 3 $\beta$ = 1 The sliders show how the same vector is reconstructed from the two chosen basis directions. Solving for the basis coordinates Using the Cartesian coordinates of the basis vectors gives \[ \begin{aligned} [4,2] &=\alpha[1,1]+\beta[1,-1]\\ &=[\alpha+\beta,\alpha-\beta]. \end{aligned} \] Therefore \[ \alpha+\beta=4, \qquad \alpha-\beta=2. \] Solving this small system gives \[ \alpha=3, \qquad \beta=1. \] Hence \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2} \] and the coordinates of the same vector in the basis \(B=(\mathbf b_1,\mathbf b_2)\) are \[ \boxed{[\mathbf v]_B=[3,1]}. \] What changes and what does not The Cartesian coordinates \([4,2]\) and the basis coordinates \([3,1]_B\) are two numerical descriptions of the same geometric vector. Changing the basis means solving for the coefficients in one concrete linear combination; no additional general machinery is introduced here.
docs.title: One vector in a different basis

docs.text: The first ten consolidate calculation and construction. The next ten require explanation, proof, comparison, or modelling. Download exercises as Markdown
docs.title: Exercises

docs.text: Let \(A=(1,2)\) and \(B=(5,-1)\) . Compute the ordinary point difference \(B-A\) and the free vector \([B-A]=[\overrightarrow{AB}]\) . Find the points \(O\oplus[B-A]\) for \(O=(0,0)\) and \(P\oplus[B-A]\) for \(P=(-2,3)\) , and draw the corresponding anchored representatives. For \(\mathbf u=[3,-2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,4]\) , compute \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , \(2\mathbf u\) , and \(-3\mathbf v\) . Draw the parallelogram for \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) . For \(\mathbf u=[1,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[2,-1]\) , compute \(2\mathbf u-3\mathbf v\) and reproduce it head to tail. Compute the lengths and unit vectors for \([3,4]\) , \([-5,12]\) , and \([2,-2]\) . Classify the pairs \([2,1],[6,3]\) ; \([2,1],[1,-2]\) ; \([3,-1],[2,5]\) as parallel, perpendicular, or neither. Find the angles between \([1,0]\) and \([1,1]\) , and between \([1,1]\) and \([-1,1]\) . For \(\mathbf u=[3,1]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,2]\) , compute \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) directly and from the cosine theorem. Project \([4,3]\) onto \([1,0]\) and onto \([1,1]\) . Verify that each remainder is perpendicular to the target direction. For \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) , and \(\mathbf u=[4,2]\) , find \([\mathbf u]_B\) and reconstruct \(\mathbf u\) . Starting from \(P=(2,-1)\) , form \((P\oplus[3,2])\oplus[-1,4]\) and then apply \(-2[3,2]\) . Find the endpoint. Combine the three free vectors first and verify that one translation \(P\oplus\bigl([3,2]+[-1,4]-2[3,2]\bigr)\) gives the same point.
docs.title: A. Elementary computational and graphical exercises

docs.text: Explain with a parallelogram why translating an arrow without changing its directed change does not change the free vector. Give a numerical example. Draw \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) for concrete vectors and explain geometrically why the endpoints coincide. Then verify that \((P\oplus\mathbf u)\oplus\mathbf v=P\oplus(\mathbf u+\mathbf v)\) . Prove that two nonzero plane vectors are parallel exactly when one is a scalar multiple of the other. Discuss positive and negative multiples separately. Find equal-length vectors with \(\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|<\|\mathbf u\|\) and explain the cancellation geometrically. Starting from the coordinate definition of the dot product, prove distributivity, homogeneity, symmetry, and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|^2\) . Use these properties to expand \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2\) and recover the cosine theorem. Let \(\mathbf r=\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\) for \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) . Prove that \(\mathbf r\perp\mathbf v\) , then use \(\|\mathbf r\|^2\geq0\) to derive the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality \(|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|\leq\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\) . Determine when equality holds. Explain why two nonparallel basis vectors give unique coefficients in \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2\) . Explain the type and meaning of each expression: \(P+Q\) , \(Q-P\) , \([Q-P]\) , \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v\) . State which expressions produce a point, a free vector, or a real number. Find the standard and \(B\) -coordinates of \([6,2]\) for \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) . Explain when a basis is well adapted. Refute the claim that every pair of nonzero vectors is a basis. Give one dependent and one independent example and interpret their linear combinations.

docs.text: Lecture 02 — Vectors, Bases, and Coordinate Systems A. Elementary computational and graphical exercises Let \(A=(1,2)\) and \(B=(5,-1)\) . Compute the ordinary point difference \(B-A\) and the free vector \([B-A]=[\overrightarrow{AB}]\) . Find the points \(O\oplus[B-A]\) for \(O=(0,0)\) and \(P\oplus[B-A]\) for \(P=(-2,3)\) , and draw the corresponding anchored representatives. For \(\mathbf u=[3,-2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,4]\) , compute \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , \(2\mathbf u\) , and \(-3\mathbf v\) . Draw \(\mathbf u\) , \(\mathbf v\) , and \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) using the parallelogram rule. For \(\mathbf u=[1,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[2,-1]\) , compute \(2\mathbf u-3\mathbf v\) . Reproduce the same displacement by placing anchored representatives head to tail. Compute the lengths and corresponding unit vectors for \(\mathbf u=[3,4]\) , \(\mathbf v=[-5,12]\) , and \(\mathbf w=[2,-2]\) . Determine which pairs are parallel, perpendicular, or neither: \([2,1]\) and \([6,3]\) ; \([2,1]\) and \([1,-2]\) ; \([3,-1]\) and \([2,5]\) . Use scalar multiples for parallelism and dot products for perpendicularity. Find the angles between \(\mathbf u=[1,0]\) and \(\mathbf v=[1,1]\) , and between \(\mathbf v=[1,1]\) and \(\mathbf w=[-1,1]\) . Draw each pair and compare the computed angle with the picture. The sides from one vertex of a triangle are \(\mathbf u=[3,1]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,2]\) . Compute \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) directly and again from the cosine theorem using the angle between \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . Compute the projection of \(\mathbf u=[4,3]\) onto \(\mathbf v=[1,0]\) and onto \(\mathbf w=[1,1]\) . In each case write \(\mathbf u\) as a parallel component plus a perpendicular remainder and verify perpendicularity by a dot product. Let \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) , and \(\mathbf u=[4,2]\) . Find \(\alpha,\beta\) such that \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2\) . State \([\mathbf u]_B\) and reconstruct \(\mathbf u\) . Starting from \(P=(2,-1)\) , form \((P\oplus[3,2])\oplus[-1,4]\) and then apply \(-2[3,2]\) . Find the endpoint. Combine the three free vectors first and verify that one translation \(P\oplus\bigl([3,2]+[-1,4]-2[3,2]\bigr)\) gives the same point. B. Development and reasoning exercises Two anchored vectors are declared equivalent when they encode the same coordinate change. Explain, using a parallelogram construction, why moving an arrow without changing that directed change does not change the free vector. Give one numerical example with two different initial points. Use three concrete vectors to draw both \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) . Explain geometrically why the endpoints coincide. Then verify that \((P\oplus\mathbf u)\oplus\mathbf v=P\oplus(\mathbf u+\mathbf v)\) . Show that two nonzero vectors in the plane are parallel exactly when one is a scalar multiple of the other. Explain separately what positive and negative scalar multiples do to orientation. Choose two nonzero vectors of equal length such that \(\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|<\|\mathbf u\|\) . Compute the lengths and explain how the angle between the vectors produces partial cancellation. Starting from the coordinate definition of the dot product, prove \[ (\mathbf u+\mathbf w)\cdot\mathbf v =\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\mathbf w\cdot\mathbf v, \qquad (\lambda\mathbf u)\cdot\mathbf v =\lambda(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v), \] together with symmetry and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|^2\) . Use these properties to expand \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2\) and recover the cosine theorem. Let \(\mathbf r=\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\) for \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) . Prove directly that \(\mathbf r\cdot\mathbf v=0\) . Then use \(\|\mathbf r\|^2\geq0\) to derive the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality \[ |\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|\leq\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|, \] and determine exactly when equality holds. Suppose \(\mathbf b_1\) and \(\mathbf b_2\) are nonparallel. Explain why a vector cannot have two different representations \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2=\alpha'\mathbf b_1+\beta'\mathbf b_2\) . Reduce the question to a linear combination equal to the zero vector. Explain the type and meaning of each expression: \(P+Q\) , \(Q-P\) , \([Q-P]\) , \(P\oplus\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , and \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v\) . State which expressions produce a point, a free vector, or a real number. The vector \(\mathbf u=[6,2]\) is awkward in the standard basis but simple in the basis \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) . Find its coordinates in both bases and explain what it means for one basis to be better adapted to a problem. A student claims that every pair of nonzero vectors forms a basis of the plane. Give one counterexample and one valid example. For each pair, explain geometrically whether its linear combinations fill a line or the whole plane.

docs.title: B. Development and reasoning exercises

docs.text:
Lines in the Cartesian plane A line should not first appear as a formula to be recognised. We already have all the ingredients needed to construct it geometrically: a point tells us where to begin, and a nonzero free vector tells us in which direction motion is allowed. The two bridge operations from Lecture 2 will be used throughout this chapter: \[ [Q-P]\in V \] is the free vector determined by two points, while \[ P\oplus\mathbf v\in\mathcal P \] is the point obtained by applying the free vector \(\mathbf v\) at \(P\) . Thus scalar multiples and sums are formed inside the vector space first, and only then applied to a point by \(\oplus\) . Guiding question. Which points can be reached from one fixed point by moving only in one fixed direction? Data that determine a line A line in the Cartesian plane is determined by one point \(P_0\) on the line and one nonzero direction vector \(\mathbf v\) . Every point of the line is reached by applying a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf v\) at \(P_0\) . Let \(P_0=(x_0,y_0)\) be fixed and let \(\mathbf v=[u,v]
eq\mathbf0\) . Every permitted displacement is the free vector \(t\mathbf v\) , so the corresponding point is \[  oxed{P=P_0\oplus t\mathbf v}, \qquad t\in\mathbb R. \] The types are explicit: \[ t\mathbf v\in V, \qquad P_0\oplus t\mathbf v\in\mathcal P. \] Parametric definition \[  oxed{L=\{P_0\oplus t\mathbf v:t\in\mathbb R\}}. \] In coordinates, \[  oxed{x=x_0+tu,\qquad y=y_0+tv.} \] Generating a line from one point and one direction Move $P_0$ and $P_0\oplus\mathbf v$, then vary $t$ to generate points of the line. ⛶ $t$ = 1 The vector $t\mathbf v$ is formed first. Applying it to $P_0$ by $\oplus$ produces the point $P(t)=P_0\oplus t\mathbf v$. At \(t=0\) we obtain \[ P_0\oplus\mathbf0=P_0. \] Positive and negative values move in the two opposite orientations. Replacing \(\mathbf v\) by a nonzero scalar multiple changes only the parametrisation, not the geometric line. The same line described by a perpendicular direction Choose a nonzero normal vector \(\mathbf n=[a,b]\) perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . For a variable point \(P=(x,y)\) , the expression \[ [P-P_0] \] is the free displacement from \(P_0\) to \(P\) . The point \(P\) lies on the line precisely when that displacement is perpendicular to \(\mathbf n\) . From direction to normal form Since both factors are free vectors, \[ \mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]=0 \] is a scalar equation. In coordinates, \[ [P-P_0]=[x-x_0,\,y-y_0], \] so \[ a(x-x_0)+b(y-y_0)=0, \] and therefore \[  oxed{ax+by=c},\qquad c=ax_0+by_0. \] The general linear equation is not a second definition. It records the same line by requiring the normal component of the displacement \([P-P_0]\) to vanish. Slope form as a special coordinate description Eliminating the parameter If \(u
eq0\) , then \(t=(x-x_0)/u\) . Substitution gives \[ y-y_0= rac vu(x-x_0),\qquad  oxed{m= rac vu}, \] and therefore \(y=mx+b\) after collecting constants. Vertical case If \(u=0\) , the line is vertical and is described by \(x=x_0\) . Parametric and normal forms need no exception; only slope notation fails. Three forms, three questions Form What it reveals \(P_0\oplus t\mathbf v\) how a permitted vector displacement produces a point of the line \(\mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]=0\) which point displacements are allowed \(y=mx+b\) how vertical position depends on horizontal position No representation is the line itself. The line is the geometric set identified by all three. What the familiar Cartesian forms let us read Choose the representation adapted to the question The parametric, normal and slope forms are not competing definitions. They expose different information about the same geometric line. One line, three descriptions Move the point and direction vector and compare the three coordinate descriptions. ⛶ The geometric line remains unchanged while the parametric, normal and slope descriptions expose different information about it. Intersections with the coordinate axes Impose both defining conditions For \(y=mx+b\) , setting \(x=0\) imposes the \(y\) -axis condition and gives \((0,b)\) . If \(m
eq0\) , setting \(y=0\) gives \(x=-b/m\) . \[  oxed{L\cap\{x=0\}=\{(0,b)\}}, \qquad  oxed{L\cap\{y=0\}=\left\{\left(- rac bm,0ight)ight\}}. \] To find an intersection of two sets, impose both defining conditions at once. Axis intercepts are the simplest example. Example For \(y=-	frac12x+3\) , the \(y\) -intercept is \((0,3)\) and the \(x\) -intercept is \((6,0)\) . The line through two points Let \[ P=(x_1,y_1),\qquad Q=(x_2,y_2) \] be distinct points. Their ordinary coordinate difference is \[ Q-P=(x_2-x_1,\,y_2-y_1), \] while the corresponding free direction vector is \[  oxed{[Q-P]=[x_2-x_1,\,y_2-y_1]
eq\mathbf0.} \] Starting at \(P\) , scalar multiples of this free vector generate the required line. Unique line through two points \[  oxed{ L=\{P\oplus t[Q-P]:t\in\mathbb R\}. } \] At \(t=0\) the point is \(P\) . At \(t=1\) , \[ P\oplus[Q-P]=Q. \] This handles vertical and nonvertical lines uniformly. Recovering slope notation If \(x_1
eq x_2\) , \[  oxed{m= rac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}}, \qquad  oxed{y-y_1=m(x-x_1)}. \] If \(x_1=x_2\) , the line is vertical: \[  oxed{x=x_1}. \] Parallel lines in slope notation Why equal slopes mean equal directions The lines \(y=m_1x+b_1\) and \(y=m_2x+b_2\) have free direction vectors \([1,m_1]\) and \([1,m_2]\) . These are parallel exactly when \(m_1=m_2\) . Parallel nonvertical lines \[  oxed{L_1\parallel L_2\iff m_1=m_2	ext{ and }b_1
eq b_2.} \] If also \(b_1=b_2\) , the equations describe the same line. Slope formulas are efficient consequences of the vector construction, but the parametric and normal descriptions are more general and extend directly to space. Vector tests behind the slope rules Direction-vector criteria \[  oxed{\mathbf v_1\parallel\mathbf v_2\iff \mathbf v_2=\lambda\mathbf v_1	ext{ for some }\lambda
eq0}, \] \[  oxed{\mathbf v_1\perp\mathbf v_2\iff \mathbf v_1\cdot\mathbf v_2=0}. \] These criteria include vertical directions without a special convention. For nonvertical lines take \(\mathbf v_1=[1,m_1]\) and \(\mathbf v_2=[1,m_2]\) . Recovering the familiar slope rule \[ \mathbf v_1\cdot\mathbf v_2=1+m_1m_2, \] so \[  oxed{L_1\perp L_2\iff m_1m_2=-1}. \] This is the slope form of the dot-product criterion, not an independent rule. The nearest point on a line Let \[ L=\{P_0\oplus t\mathbf v:t\in\mathbb R\}, \qquad \mathbf v
eq\mathbf0, \] and let \(P\) be any point. The square-bracketed difference \[ [P-P_0] \] is the free displacement from \(P_0\) to \(P\) . Projecting onto the allowed direction Decompose \([P-P_0]\) into a component parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and a perpendicular component: \[ \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}[P-P_0] = rac{[P-P_0]\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\,\mathbf v. \] The projection is a free vector. Applying it at \(P_0\) reaches the foot \(Q\) : \[  oxed{ Q=P_0\oplus\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}[P-P_0]. } \] Nearest point and distance to a line Move $P$, $P_0$, and the line direction. ⛶ The free remainder $[P-Q]$ is perpendicular to the permitted direction of the line. The perpendicular remainder is \[ [P-P_0]-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}[P-P_0]. \] Distance in parametric form \[  oxed{ d(P,L) =\left\|[P-P_0]-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}[P-P_0]ight\|. } \] Every other route from \(P\) to the line contains the same perpendicular component plus an additional component along the line. The perpendicular component alone is shortest. Distance from the normal equation Suppose \(L:ax+by=c\) with normal \(\mathbf n=[a,b]\) . Let \(P=(x_P,y_P)\) and let \(P_0=(x_0,y_0)\) be any point on the line. Reading the normal component The displacement from \(P_0\) to \(P\) is \[ [P-P_0]=[x_P-x_0,\,y_P-y_0]. \] Therefore \[  egin{aligned} \mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0] &=a(x_P-x_0)+b(y_P-y_0)\ &=ax_P+by_P-c. \end{aligned} \] The signed scalar component along the unit normal is \[  rac{ax_P+by_P-c}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}. \] Distance is its absolute value. Point-to-line distance \[  oxed{d(P,L)= rac{|ax_P+by_P-c|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}}. \] The numerator measures signed displacement in the normal direction; the denominator normalises the normal vector. Planes in three-dimensional space A line was produced by allowing one real parameter, \[ P=P_0\oplus t\mathbf v. \] Only one independent direction of motion was available. Guiding question. What set is obtained when motion from one fixed point is allowed in two independent directions? Let \(P_0\in\mathcal P\) be a point in three-dimensional space and let \(\mathbf u,\mathbf v\in V\) be nonparallel free vectors. Generating the plane Every permitted displacement is a linear combination \[ s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v\in V. \] Applying that free vector at \(P_0\) gives the point \[  oxed{ P=P_0\oplus(s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v). } \] One parameter produces a line; two independent parameters produce a plane. If the directions were parallel, every combination would still lie along one direction. Parametric plane \[  oxed{ \Pi=\{P_0\oplus(s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v):s,t\in\mathbb R\}. } \] If \(P_0=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) , \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2,u_3]\) , and \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2,v_3]\) , then \[  oxed{  egin{cases} x=x_0+su_1+tv_1,\ y=y_0+su_2+tv_2,\ z=z_0+su_3+tv_3. \end{cases}} \] Two independent directions generate a plane Change the two vector coefficients and rotate the scene. ⛶ $s$ = 1 $t$ = 1 The combination $s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v$ is a free displacement. The point $P_0\oplus(s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v)$ is obtained by applying that displacement at $P_0$. A normal vector from two directions The plane is generated by two free directions. To write one scalar equation for it, we need a free vector perpendicular to both. In three-dimensional space the cross product is an additional vector operation: \[ V	imes V\longrightarrow V, \qquad (\mathbf u,\mathbf v)\longmapsto\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v. \] Cross product For \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2,u_3]\) and \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2,v_3]\) , \[  oxed{\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v=[u_2v_3-u_3v_2,\;u_3v_1-u_1v_3,\;u_1v_2-u_2v_1]}. \] Direct substitution gives \[ (\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf u=0, \qquad (\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf v=0. \] Thus \(\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v\) is perpendicular to both generating directions and may be used as a normal. Reversing the order reverses the normal, \[ \mathbf v	imes\mathbf u=-(\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v), \] but both normals describe the same plane. From the normal direction to an equation Let \[ \mathbf n=\mathbf u	imes\mathbf v. \] A variable point \(P\) belongs to the plane through \(P_0\) precisely when the free displacement \[ [P-P_0] \] lies in the span of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . Since \(\mathbf n\) is perpendicular to both generating directions, \[  oxed{\mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]=0}. \] Both factors of the dot product are free vectors, so the left-hand side is a real number. For \(P=(x,y,z)\) , \(P_0=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) , and \(\mathbf n=[a,b,c]\) , \[ [P-P_0]=[x-x_0,\,y-y_0,\,z-z_0], \] hence \[ a(x-x_0)+b(y-y_0)+c(z-z_0)=0, \] or \[  oxed{ax+by+cz=d}. \] Two descriptions, one plane \[  oxed{P=P_0\oplus(s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v)}, \qquad  oxed{\mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]=0\iff ax+by+cz=d}. \] The parametric equation first forms an allowed free displacement and applies it to \(P_0\) . The normal equation requires the forbidden normal component of \([P-P_0]\) to vanish. A plane through three points Extracting two independent directions Let \(P,Q,R\) be three noncollinear points. Their ordinary coordinate differences are points, while the corresponding free direction vectors are \[ \mathbf u=[Q-P], \qquad \mathbf v=[R-P]. \] Noncollinearity means that these two free vectors are nonparallel. Their cross product \[ \mathbf n=[Q-P]	imes[R-P] \] is nonzero and perpendicular to the plane. Unique plane through three noncollinear points \[  oxed{ \Pi= \left\{ P\oplus igl(s[Q-P]+t[R-P] igr):s,t\in\mathbb R ight\}. } \] \[  oxed{\mathbf n=[Q-P]	imes[R-P].} \] The two expressions have the correct types: the quantity inside the parentheses is a free vector, and applying it at \(P\) produces a point of the plane. Example For \[ P=(1,0,0),\qquad Q=(0,1,0),\qquad R=(0,0,1), \] the ordinary point differences are \[ Q-P=(-1,1,0), \qquad R-P=(-1,0,1), \] and the corresponding free vectors are \[ [Q-P]=[-1,1,0], \qquad [R-P]=[-1,0,1]. \] Therefore \[ [Q-P]	imes[R-P]=[1,1,1]. \] Let \(X=(x,y,z)\) be a variable point. Since \[ [X-P]=[x-1,\,y,\,z], \] the normal equation is \[ [1,1,1]\cdot[X-P]=0, \] so \[  oxed{x+y+z=1}. \] Noncollinearity is essential. If \(P,Q,R\) are collinear, then \([Q-P]\) and \([R-P]\) are parallel and their cross product is zero, so they do not supply two independent directions. Relative positions of planes Let \(P_1\) and \(P_2\) be fixed points on two planes and let \(\mathbf n_1,\mathbf n_2\) be their nonzero normal vectors. Write \[ \Pi_1=\{P:\mathbf n_1\cdot[P-P_1]=0\}, \qquad \Pi_2=\{P:\mathbf n_2\cdot[P-P_2]=0\}. \] Here \([P-P_i]\) is a free displacement and each dot product is a real number. Normals control relative position If the normals are parallel, the planes have the same orientation and are either identical or distinct and parallel. If the normals are not parallel, the planes intersect in a line. Angle and intersection direction \[  oxed{\Pi_1\perp\Pi_2\iff\mathbf n_1\cdot\mathbf n_2=0}, \] and, when the planes are nonparallel, \[  oxed{\mathbf v=\mathbf n_1	imes\mathbf n_2} \] is a free direction vector of their intersection line. The cross product gives the direction because the intersection line must be perpendicular to both normals. A point \(P_0\) on the line is found by solving both scalar plane equations simultaneously. The intersection line is then \[  oxed{ L=\{P_0\oplus t(\mathbf n_1	imes\mathbf n_2):t\in\mathbb R\}. } \] Normals determine how planes meet Compare the three spatial configurations and drag the scene to rotate it. ⛶ Intersecting Parallel Perpendicular The planes, their normal vectors, and the intersection line are displayed in a rotatable three-dimensional scene. Distance from a point to a plane Distance as a normal projection Let \(P_0\) lie on the plane and let \(P\) be any point. The free displacement from \(P_0\) to \(P\) is \([P-P_0]\) . Its scalar component in the normal direction is \[  rac{\mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]}{\|\mathbf n\|}. \] Therefore \[  oxed{ d(P,\Pi)= rac{|\mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]|}{\|\mathbf n\|}. } \] Point-to-plane distance For \(ax+by+cz=d\) and \(P=(x_P,y_P,z_P)\) , \[  oxed{d(P,\Pi)= rac{|ax_P+by_P+cz_P-d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}}. \] The numerator is signed normal displacement; the denominator converts the chosen normal to unit length. Line and plane from permitted directions A line is generated by one independent free direction, \[ P=P_0\oplus t\mathbf v, \] whereas a plane is generated by two, \[ P=P_0\oplus(s\mathbf u+t\mathbf v). \] The cross product produces a normal, and the dot product turns perpendicularity into the scalar equation \[ \mathbf n\cdot[P-P_0]=0. \] Practice and development The first ten exercises are computational and graphical. The next ten require explanation, derivation, or comparison of representations.
